topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to bleed into the stark white of the desk. Elias stared at the C-minus, a verdict that felt heavier than any textbook he’d ever carried. His shoulders slumped, the tension draining out of him until he felt strangely light, almost hollowed out. He traced the curve of the grade with a fingertip, the movement slow and deliberate. A low, involuntary sound escaped his throat—a soft, breathy sigh that held no real air. He pushed the paper away, the sound of the movement sharp in the quiet room. He didn't bother to look at the notes he’d studied; the effort felt pointless now. He just sat there, letting the weight of the disappointing result settle deep in his chest, a dull, persistent ache that made his limbs feel sluggish.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" Maya whispered, the word barely audible as she stared at the transcript. Her jaw tightened, the muscles in her neck corded with frustration. She picked up the printed report, her fingers trembling slightly before she let it fall back onto the wooden surface. A wave of heat washed over her face, quickly replaced by a cold, empty sensation spreading through her core. She began picking at a loose thread on her sleeve, her gaze fixed on the tiny imperfection as if it held the answer. Every sound in the hallway seemed amplified—the distant chatter, the squeak of a chair—and she found herself retreating into a quiet corner of her mind, cataloging the failure, the perceived inadequacy, with a meticulous, almost detached precision.

[story 3]

I reread the feedback, the professor’s notes a blur of harsh critiques. My hands felt clumsy, thick, as if they were filled with sand. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest. The world outside the window seemed muted, the colors drained from the landscape. I didn't move to tidy my desk; the effort seemed monumental. Instead, I just let my eyes drift over the room—the scattered papers, the unopened water bottle—each object a testament to the unfinished business, the failure. A profound stillness settled over me, a heavy blanket muffling any desire to engage with the reality of the situation. It was easier, in that moment, just to exist in the quiet aftermath of the letdown.
